description:
Week-5 of the HCL Java CDC program covers Code Quality, Security, and Full-Stack Integration.

Sections:
1. Code Coverage Techniques and Tools (2 Hours)
2. SonarQube Implementation
3. Full-Stack Integration (8 Hours)
4. Security Tools
5. Deployment

Faculty: Dr. SUVIDHA RUPESH KUMAR — suvidha.rupesh@vit.ac.in

Goal: Write testable, high-quality code. Measure and improve code coverage. Connect React frontend to Spring Boot backend. Deploy the complete application.

Duration: 2 Hours

WHAT IS CODE COVERAGE?
Code coverage = percentage of source code lines/branches/paths executed by tests.
High coverage does NOT mean tests are good — but low coverage guarantees gaps.

HOW IS IT MEASURED?
Tools instrument bytecode at compile time to record which lines execute during tests.
Reports show: covered (green), partially covered (yellow), not covered (red).

COVERAGE TYPES:
  Line Coverage     — % of lines executed by tests (most basic)
  Branch Coverage   — % of decision branches (if/else, switch cases) tested
  Method Coverage   — % of methods called
  Class Coverage    — % of classes instantiated
  Instruction Coverage — % of JVM bytecode instructions (most granular)
  Path Coverage     — every possible execution path (exponential — impractical)

CODE COVERAGE vs TEST COVERAGE:
  Code coverage: which lines ran
  Test coverage: which requirements/scenarios are tested
  A line can be executed without being properly asserted → 100% coverage, 0% confidence

COVERAGE TARGETS:
  Industry standard: 70-80% for production code
  Don't chase 100%: getters, setters, boilerplate, config classes are low value
  Focus on: business logic, edge cases, error paths

COVERAGE TOOLS:
  JaCoCo — Java Code Coverage (most popular for Spring Boot)
    Add to pom.xml: jacoco-maven-plugin
    mvn test jacoco:report → generates HTML report in target/site/jacoco/

  SonarQube — integrates JaCoCo, adds code quality analysis
  IntelliJ IDEA — built-in coverage runner (Run with Coverage)
  Cobertura — legacy Java coverage tool
  Istanbul/nyc — JavaScript/Node.js coverage
  pytest-cov — Python coverage

Faculty: Dr. SUVIDHA RUPESH KUMAR

WHAT IS SONARQUBE?
Static code analysis platform — finds bugs, vulnerabilities, code smells, and duplications before they reach production.

SONARQUBE METRICS:
  Bugs            — actual or likely runtime errors
  Vulnerabilities — security issues (SQL injection, XSS, hardcoded passwords)
  Code Smells     — maintainability issues (long methods, dead code, complex logic)
  Duplications    — copy-pasted code blocks
  Technical Debt  — estimated time to fix all issues
  Security Hotspots — code to review for security
  Coverage        — from JaCoCo integration

SONARQUBE ARCHITECTURE:
  SonarQube Server (web, compute engine, search)
  Database (stores analysis results)
  SonarScanner (runs in CI, sends results to server)

DOCKERIZED SETUP:
  docker run -d --name sonarqube \
    -p 9000:9000 \
    sonarqube:latest
  # Access: http://localhost:9000 (admin/admin)

MAVEN PROJECT SCANNING:
  // pom.xml — add sonar properties
  <properties>
    <sonar.projectKey>my-project</sonar.projectKey>
    <sonar.host.url>http://localhost:9000</sonar.host.url>
    <sonar.login>your-token</sonar.login>
  </properties>

  mvn clean verify sonar:sonar  // scan + report

SONARQUBE QUALITY GATE:
  Pass/Fail threshold for:
  • Coverage > 80%
  • No new critical bugs
  • No new vulnerabilities
  • Duplication < 3%
  Integrate with CI — fail build if Quality Gate fails

NODE.JS / FRONTEND SCANNING:
  sonar-scanner -Dsonar.projectKey=react-app \
    -Dsonar.sources=src \
    -Dsonar.javascript.lcov.reportPaths=coverage/lcov.info

SSL SETUP:
  Generate SSL cert → configure nginx reverse proxy → sonarqube behind HTTPS
  Used in production environments

TRIVY — Container & Filesystem Vulnerability Scanner:
  Scans Docker images, filesystems, and Git repos for:
  • CVEs in OS packages and application dependencies
  • Misconfigurations (Dockerfile, Kubernetes YAML)
  • Exposed secrets

  trivy image openjdk:17                    # scan Docker image
  trivy fs ./                               # scan filesystem
  trivy repo https://github.com/org/repo   # scan git repo

  Integrate in CI:
  - name: Trivy scan
    run: trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL my-app:latest
  # Fails build if CRITICAL vulnerability found

OWASP DEPENDENCY-CHECK:
  Scans Java (Maven/Gradle) and Node.js dependencies for known CVEs.
  OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities to know:
    A01: Broken Access Control
    A02: Cryptographic Failures
    A03: Injection (SQL, LDAP, XSS)
    A07: Identification/Authentication Failures
    A08: Software and Data Integrity Failures

  Maven integration:
  <plugin>
    <groupId>org.owasp</groupId>
    <artifactId>dependency-check-maven</artifactId>
    <version>9.0.0</version>
  </plugin>
  mvn dependency-check:check

PROWLER — Cloud Security Tool:
  Open-source cloud security assessment tool for AWS, Azure, GCP.
  Checks: IAM policies, S3 bucket permissions, security groups, encryption.
  prowler aws --profile default --region ap-south-1
  Generates HTML report with findings by severity.

DOCKLE — Docker Image Linter:
  Checks Dockerfile for security best practices:
  • Running as non-root user
  • Removing shell
  • Using specific image versions (not :latest)
  • Minimising layer count
  dockle my-app:latest

DEPLOYMENT STRATEGY FOR FULL-STACK JAVA APP:

FRONTEND (React):
  Build: npm run build  → dist/ folder (static HTML/CSS/JS)
  Deploy to:
  • Netlify: drag-and-drop or connect GitHub repo — auto-deploys on push
  • Vercel: git push → automatic build + CDN deployment
  • GitHub Pages: for static sites
  • AWS S3 + CloudFront: production-grade CDN

BACKEND (Spring Boot):
  Build: mvn clean package  → target/app.jar
  Deploy to:
  • Railway (free tier): connect GitHub, auto-deploys Spring Boot
  • Render: free tier, Docker or Java support
  • Heroku: easy PaaS, but now paid
  • AWS Elastic Beanstalk: managed PaaS on AWS
  • Docker + VPS: full control

VERSION CONTROL WORKFLOW:
  main branch — production code only
  develop branch — integration branch
  feature/login — feature branches
  Flow: feature → PR → code review → merge to develop → test → merge to main → deploy

MINI PROJECT DEPLOYMENT CHECKLIST:
  □ Frontend: npm run build, deploy to Netlify
  □ Backend: jar deployed to Railway/Render
  □ Database: Railway MySQL or PlanetScale
  □ Environment variables: never commit secrets
  □ CORS: production frontend URL whitelisted in Spring Boot
  □ JWT secret: stored in environment variable, not code
  □ GitHub Actions: auto-deploy on push to main

SAMPLE GITHUB ACTIONS — Full Deploy:
  jobs:
    deploy-backend:
      runs-on: ubuntu-latest
      steps:
        - mvn clean package
        - docker build + push to Docker Hub
        - SSH deploy to server (or Railway webhook)
    deploy-frontend:
      - npm run build
      - netlify deploy --prod --dir=dist
